sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at row 28-31, col 40-43, to the right of the center bar. The bar has restored to its original 4x12 state. Player is close to the right frame at col 44-47.
- Goal model: Still exploring. Have visited all 3 frames and traversed the bar. Need to find the win condition.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to enter the right frame and see what happens.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the player (green body) is now to the right of the right frame. The right frame is at col 44-47, and the player appears to be at col 44-47 or slightly to the right. Let me check the segmentation to see the exact position and frame colors.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Player position:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nFrames:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is at col 44-47, overlapping with the right frame's column range. The right frame is at row 24-27, col 44-47, and the player is at row 28-31, col 44-47. So the player is below the right frame, in the same column range. The frame is still 'c', not 'G'.
Wait, but earlier when the player was below the right frame, it became 'G'. Let me check if the frame color changed. The frame is still 'c'.
Hmm, but earlier when the player was at row 28-31, col 44-47 (below the right frame), the frame became 'G'. Now the player is at the same position, but the frame is still 'c'.
Actually, let me re-check. Earlier, the player was at row 28-31, col 44-47, and the right frame (at row 24-27, col 44-47) became 'G'. But now the player is at the same position, and the frame is 'c'.
Wait, maybe the frame only becomes 'G' when the player is adjacent AND the player is in a specific orientation or state. Or maybe the frame color depends on something else.
Actually, let me re-examine the earlier evidence. When the player was at row 28-31, col 44-47, the right frame became 'G'. But then when the player moved away (to the center), the frame reverted to 'c'. Now the player is back at col 44-47, but the frame is still 'c'.
Wait, maybe the frame color depends on the player's row position relative to the frame. The right frame is at row 24-27, and the player is at row 28-31. The gap is 1 row (row 27 is frame bottom, row 28 is player top).
Actually, I think I need to reconsider. Let me check if the player is actually at row 28-31 or if the player's row changed when moving along the bar.
Looking at the segmentation, the player is at row 28-31, col 44-47. The right frame is at row 24-27, col 44-47. The player is below the frame.
But the frame is 'c', not 'G'. This is different from earlier when the player was below the right frame and it became 'G'.
Wait, let me re-check the earlier evidence. In the first exploration, the player started at row 28-31, col 32-35 (center-below). Then the player moved RIGHT to col 44-47, and the right frame became 'G'. But now the player is at the same position, and the frame is 'c'.
The difference might be that earlier, the player was at row 28-31, col 44-47, and the frame was at row 24-27, col 44-47. The player was below the frame. But now, the player is at row 28-31, col 44-47, and the frame is at row 24-27, col 44-47. The player is still below the frame.
Hmm, maybe the frame color depends on something else, like the player's orientation or the order of visits.
Actually, let me try moving UP to enter the right frame and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at row 28-31, col 44-47, below the right frame (row 24-27, col 44-47). Frame is still 'c'. Earlier, when the player was at this position, the frame was 'G', but now it's 'c'. The frame color behavior may have changed.
Goal model: Still exploring. Need to understand the frame activation mechanic better.
Plan: Move UP to enter the right frame and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move UP to enter the right frame
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
